original_abstract:
While conventional development models suggest that emerging economies only begin to produce original technology at the end of their growth trajectory, India and China have defied this pattern by generating an explosion of U.S. patents while still at relatively low income levels. This paper investigates the nature of this innovative surge to determine if it truly challenges traditional views of global technological leadership. Using USPTO data and qualitative interviews, we find that the rapid patent growth in these regions is largely driven by multinational corporations (MNCs) and is highly dependent on international "coinvention"—collaborative research between local inventors and those in advanced economies.

Our empirical analysis of patent quality, measured by forward citations, reveals a burgeoning international division of R&D labor. We demonstrate that MNC-sponsored and coinvented patents are typically of higher quality than those produced by indigenous firms, yet they often remain less impactful than the inventions produced by the same MNCs in their home countries. This suggests a vertical disintegration of the R&D process, where routine or adaptive tasks are offshored while fundamental breakthroughs still rely on Western intellectual input. While we find evidence that this quality gap is narrowing in China, the disparity remains persistent in India. Ultimately, our findings suggest that rather than undermining Western leadership, the rise of innovation in India and China represents a complementary evolution of the global R&D network, offering a "win-win" scenario for global technological advancement.
humanized_abstract:
The conventional wisdom of the development literature is that developing countries can only start to generate their own technological innovations towards the end of the development process. But India and China are producing an explosion of patents across a whole range of areas of science and technology while their incomes are still low. In this paper, we ask whether this reflects a paradigm shift in the world leadership in technology. We combine analysis of trends in patenting at USPTO, as well as evidence derived from interviews, to understand the source of rapid patent growth and find that a large part of the activity is driven by multinational corporations and it is coinvented by technologists in developing countries and MNCs working together.

The empirical evidence for our measure of patent quality — forward citations — points to a burgeoning international division of R&D labor. Foreign, MNC-sponsored, and coinvented patents tend to be of higher quality than domestic firm patents but always are less heavily cited than the MNC’s own domestic patents. This indicates a form of vertical disintegration of the R&D process where companies tend to send routine or adaptive work offshore and keep only fundamental discoveries in the core. While our analysis indicates that the quality gap is beginning to close in China, it has not begun to close in India. This does not mean that the emerging competition with Western powers in basic science will be a losing proposition for the West. Rather than threatening the dominance of Western innovators, the development of India and China as centres of scientific innovation is a complementary factor in the increasingly globalised R&D system and it will enable a win-win outcome for the development of technology all over the world.
